redis.call.description: Executes a Redis command from within a Lua script and returns the result. If the command returns an error, redis.call raises a Lua error that aborts the script execution immediately.

redis.pcall.description: Executes a Redis command from within a Lua script in protected mode. Unlike redis.call, errors are caught and returned as Lua tables with an err field instead of aborting the script.

redis.log.description: Writes a log message to the Redis server log from within a Lua script. Log levels include redis.LOG_DEBUG, redis.LOG_VERBOSE, redis.LOG_NOTICE, and redis.LOG_WARNING.

redis.error_reply.description: Creates an error reply table that can be returned from a Lua script to the Redis client, formatted as a Redis error response with the specified error message string.

redis.status_reply.description: Creates a status reply table that can be returned from a Lua script to the Redis client, formatted as a Redis simple status response like OK or QUEUED.

KEYS.description: Returns all keys matching the specified glob-style pattern. Supports wildcards: * matches any sequence, ? matches one character, and [ae] matches character classes. Avoid in production due to O(N) scan.

TYPE.description: Returns the data type of the value stored at the given key as a string: string, list, set, zset, hash, stream, or none if the key does not exist in the database.

TTL.description: Returns the remaining time to live of a key that has a timeout set, in whole seconds. Returns -1 if the key exists but has no expiry, or -2 if the key does not exist.

PTTL.description: Returns the remaining time to live of a key in milliseconds for higher precision than TTL. Returns -1 for keys without expiry and -2 for non-existent keys.

EXPIRE.description: Sets a timeout on a key in seconds after which it will be automatically deleted. Returns 1 if set successfully, 0 if key does not exist. Supports NX, XX, GT, LT flags in Redis 7.0+.

PERSIST.description: Removes the existing timeout on a key, making it persistent so it will not be automatically deleted. Returns 1 if the timeout was removed, 0 if the key has no associated timeout.

SCAN.signature: SCAN cursor [MATCH pattern] [COUNT count] [TYPE type] -> [cursor, Array<string>]
SCAN.description: Incrementally iterates over the key space using a cursor-based approach, returning a new cursor and a batch of keys. Production-safe alternative to KEYS for large databases without blocking.

EVAL.description: Evaluates a Lua script server-side with atomic execution guarantee. The script receives keys via KEYS table and arguments via ARGV table. All Redis commands within the script are atomic.

EVALSHA.description: Evaluates a previously loaded Lua script by its SHA1 hash, avoiding retransmission of the script body. Use SCRIPT LOAD to cache the script and obtain its SHA1 digest.

SCRIPT LOAD.description: Loads a Lua script into the Redis script cache without executing it, returning the SHA1 digest of the script. The cached script can then be executed with EVALSHA for reduced bandwidth.

CONFIG GET.description: Retrieves the value of a Redis configuration parameter at runtime. Supports glob-style patterns to retrieve multiple parameters at once for server introspection and diagnostics.

CONFIG SET.signature: CONFIG SET parameter value [parameter value ...] -> string
CONFIG SET.description: Dynamically reconfigures a Redis server parameter at runtime without restart. Not all parameters are modifiable; some require a server restart to take effect from the configuration file.

INFO.description: Returns detailed information and statistics about the Redis server in a human-readable format. Sections include server, clients, memory, stats, replication, cpu, keyspace, and modules.

MULTI.description: Marks the start of a Redis transaction block. Subsequent commands are queued for atomic execution when EXEC is called. All commands in the transaction execute sequentially without interruption.

EXEC.description: Executes all previously queued commands in a MULTI transaction block atomically and returns an array of replies, one for each command in the order they were queued.
